raw:
{
  "weights": {
    "R1.1": 0.0,
    "R1.2": 0.7,
    "R1.3": 0.7,
    "R1.4": 0.0,
    "R1.5": 0.0,
    "R1.6": 0.7,
    "R1.7": 0.0,
    "RX.1": 1.0,
    "RX.2": 1.0,
    "RX.3": 1.0,
    "RX.4": 1.0,
    "RX.5": 1.0
  },
  "active_set": ["R1.2", "R1.3", "R1.6", "RX.1", "RX.2", "RX.3", "RX.4", "RX.5"],
  "rationale": {
    "R1.2": "The methods section describes a rigorous procedure (Wilcoxon signed-rank + Holm correction, 'non-trivial' statistics across all four pairings), yet the interpretation paragraphs hedge so severely that the conclusions appear weaker than the stated evidence warrants — a material logical consistency gap between premises and conclusions.",
    "R1.3": "Quantitative results ('non-trivial signed-rank statistics in all four pairings,' majority-of-queries wins) are never actually cited in the interpretive paragraphs; instead, stacked hedges substitute for the evidence, creating misalignment between the data reported and the claims drawn from it.",
    "R1.6": "The prose is saturated with compounded hedge phrases ('may perhaps offer some degree,' 'arguably somewhat modest,' 'could conceivably be sensitive,' 'more or less stable') that far exceed calibrated epistemic caution and impair both clarity and professional register."
  },
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "Is your primary concern with the writing style and tone — i.e., you want the hedging language revised for clarity and appropriate confidence — or with whether the interpretive conclusions logically follow from the statistical evidence that was reported?"
}

rationale.R1.2: The methods section describes a rigorous procedure (Wilcoxon signed-rank + Holm correction, 'non-trivial' statistics across all four pairings), yet the interpretation paragraphs hedge so severely that the conclusions appear weaker than the stated evidence warrants — a material logical consistency gap between premises and conclusions.
rationale.R1.3: Quantitative results ('non-trivial signed-rank statistics in all four pairings,' majority-of-queries wins) are never actually cited in the interpretive paragraphs; instead, stacked hedges substitute for the evidence, creating misalignment between the data reported and the claims drawn from it.
rationale.R1.6: The prose is saturated with compounded hedge phrases ('may perhaps offer some degree,' 'arguably somewhat modest,' 'could conceivably be sensitive,' 'more or less stable') that far exceed calibrated epistemic caution and impair both clarity and professional register.

parsed.clarification_question: Is your primary concern with the writing style and tone — i.e., you want the hedging language revised for clarity and appropriate confidence — or with whether the interpretive conclusions logically follow from the statistical evidence that was reported?
